title: Faithfulness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: What Faithfulness Is
subpoints: Not faith (the ability to believe) but being worthy of belief — trustworthy, loyal, dependable
Being a 'China-kind' person — one others entrust their most breakable things to
The same faithfulness Paul had in mind in Galatians 5 is what Hebrews 3 describes in Christ

outline.title: The Faithfulness of Christ: Greater Than Moses
subpoints: God testified that Moses was faithful in all His house (Numbers 12:7)
Jesus is greater than Moses because His mission was more difficult, more significant, and accomplished perfectly
Moses failed and could not enter the Promised Land; Jesus finished His mission without misgivings

outline.title: Three Aspects of Christ's Faithfulness (John 17)
subpoints: Christ revealed God's name to His people (vv. 6, 12, 26) — God's nature and being made known in flesh
Christ preached the Father's Word to His people (vv. 8, 14) — even though it made them want to kill Him
Christ kept the Father's people (v. 12) — none lost except the son of perdition; continues keeping them by His Spirit

outline.title: The Assurance Christ's Faithfulness Provides
subpoints: The only condition to benefit from Christ's faithfulness is to receive it (John 6:37)
Even if we are faithless, He remains faithful — He cannot deny Himself (2 Timothy 2:13; Malachi 3:6)
Christ's faithfulness is as infinite as He is — nothing can separate us from His love (Romans 8)

outline.title: Our Response: Living Faithfully
subpoints: Gratitude — and in gratitude, being faithful as well
The same Spirit who anointed Jesus lives in us and enables our faithfulness
Faithfulness to God: committed to worship, family devotions, prayer, submitting to God's direction
Faithfulness to others: spouses, children, friends, church members, prayer for one another

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on faithfulness as the seventh fruit of the Spirit, establishing that faithfulness is not the ability to believe but being worthy of belief — the quality of being trustworthy, loyal, and dependable, like a person you would trust with your most precious and breakable possessions. Beginning with Hebrews 3:1-15 and its comparison of Christ to Moses, he shows that Jesus is more faithful than Moses because His mission — the redemption of His people — was infinitely more difficult and significant, and He accomplished it without reluctance or failure. Moving to John 17, he identifies three dimensions of Christ's faithfulness: He revealed God's name (His nature and being) to His people, He preached the Father's Word even at the cost of His life, and He kept all those the Father gave Him so that none were lost. This leads to a rich treatment of the assurance of salvation: Christ's faithfulness has no expiration date, He cannot deny Himself, and nothing can separate His people from His love. The sermon closes by pressing the practical implications — the same Spirit who enabled Christ's faithfulness lives in every believer, and we are called to reflect that faithfulness in our commitment to God and to the specific people He has placed in our lives: spouses, children, friends, and fellow church members.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Hebrews 3:1-15

If you're able, you may stand for a moment longer. We turn to the book of Hebrews chapter 3. We're going to read verses 1 through 15. This is the word of our Lord:

"Therefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the Apostle and High Priest of our confession, Christ Jesus, who was faithful to Him who appointed Him, as Moses also was faithful in all His house. For this One has been counted worthy of more glory than Moses, inasmuch as He who built the house has more honor than the house. For every house is built by someone, but He who built all things is God. And Moses indeed was faithful in all His house as a servant, for a testimony of those things which would be spoken afterward, but Christ as a Son over His own house, whose house we are if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm to the end.

"Therefore, as the Holy Spirit says: 'Today, if you will hear His voice, do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion, in the day of trial in the wilderness, where your fathers tested Me, tried Me, and saw My works forty years. Therefore I was angry with that generation, and said, "They always go astray in their heart, and they have not known My ways." So I swore in My wrath, "They shall not enter My rest."'

"Beware, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief in departing from the living God; but exhort one another daily, while it is called "Today," lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin. For we have become partakers of Christ if we hold the beginning of our confidence steadfast to the end, while it is said: 'Today, if you will hear His voice, do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion.'"

## Introduction

We continue our series on the fruit of the Spirit. In Galatians 5:22-23, the Apostle says: "The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control." Today we arrive at the seventh fruit: faithfulness.

And as with all the other fruits listed in Galatians 5, faithfulness starts with God. I hope by now we have recognized the pattern of these sermons.

Paul, when you read Galatians 5, is unquestionably telling us that we are to be faithful in the context of the local church — faithful to one another. But to know what faithfulness looks like, we must first look at Jesus and His faithfulness to us.

## What Faithfulness Is

The faithfulness referred to in Hebrews is the same faithfulness Paul had in mind with the seventh fruit of the Spirit. It is not our ability to believe — it is not faith in that sense. It is being a person worth believing in. It is being somebody who is trustworthy, who is loyal, who can be counted on.

We used this illustration in Sunday School: being a "China-kind" person — someone others trust with the most precious, breakable things of their life. That is what a faithful person is. One who keeps commitments so that people can put their faith in you. No one exemplifies all these characteristics of faithfulness better than our Lord Jesus Christ.

## Christ Is More Faithful Than Moses

If you are a true believer, the book of Hebrews will show you that Christ is better than many things — better than the angels, the Old Testament prophets, the sacrificial system, the Levitical priesthood. And in our passage here, He is even better and greater than Moses.

Moses was faithful — and God says so. In Numbers 12:7, comparing Moses to other prophets, God says: "Not so with my servant Moses. He is faithful in all My house." But Moses' faithfulness is just a shadow of Christ's faithfulness.

Jesus is greater than Moses because His mission was more difficult and more significant. What is more difficult than leading millions of complaining people in the desert for forty years? Your salvation. Unlike Moses, Jesus took on His mission without any misgivings and carried it out perfectly to the end. Moses is not allowed to go into the Promised Land because he failed to be faithful to the Lord. Jesus succeeded to the end.

His mission was the redemption of His people — carried out through His perfect, law-fulfilling life and selfless, curse-bearing death. He lived a perfect life. Everything God expected of a human being, Jesus fulfilled — even though He didn't need to, being already perfect in Himself. He did that because He knew there was a group of people He loved very much who were not going to be able to keep God's law perfectly. And that group is you. When you believed in Jesus, God the Father counted that perfect life of Christ to be yours.

But that was not enough. Because we have sinned. Someone had to pay. Jesus said: I will go. I will be separated from the Father, so that my people are never separated from Him. He willingly died on the cross — physically excruciating, but even more spiritually excruciating, because He who had never been separated from His Father in all eternity was separated from Him on that Friday afternoon. So that your communion with the Father could be perfect for all eternity.

## Three Aspects of Christ's Faithfulness: John 17

In John 17, just a few hours before His betrayal and arrest, the Son cries out to the Father in prayer. This passage shows us three aspects of what Christ did that proves His faithfulness.

**First, Christ revealed God's name to His people** (vv. 6, 12, 26). "I have manifested Your name to the men whom You have given Me." Apart from God revealing Himself to us, we could never know Him — He transcends our existence entirely. Our Confession says that the distance between God and the creature is so great that finite minds cannot comprehend the infinite. But God was faithful to Himself and chose to reveal Himself in a way we can understand — in flesh and blood, in the Lord Jesus Christ. "No one has seen God at any time. The only begotten Son... He has declared Him" (John 1:18).

**Second, Christ preached the Father's Word to His people** (vv. 8, 14). "I have given to them the words which You gave Me, and they have received them." Christ gave His people the Word of God, remaining faithful in preaching the truth that leads to eternal life even though it made people want to kill Him (John 5:18).

**Third, Christ kept the Father's people** (v. 12). "While I was with them in the world, I kept them in Your name. Those whom You gave Me I have kept; and none of them is lost except the son of perdition." Christ continually kept His disciples, and He continues to keep His people today through the ministry of His Spirit.

The opening words of His prayer reflect His completed work: "I have finished the work which You have given Me to do." There is nothing else that you and I have to do to accomplish our salvation. Jesus has finished it all.

## Assurance: The Fine Print of Christ's Faithfulness

At some point, nagging questions will creep into the sin-stricken and doubting heart. Are there conditions to enjoy His faithfulness? What qualifications must I meet? Can I lose it?

Jesus preemptively answers: "All that the Father gives Me will come to Me, and the one who comes to Me I will by no means cast out" (John 6:37). You are in the hand of Christ and there is nothing that can remove you from there. Romans 8 tells us that nothing — not angels, not circumstances, not even your own heart — can separate you from the love of God in Christ Jesus.

It is this promise by the faithful Savior, and not any promise of faith we could ever make, that qualifies us to receive the unfading love of God. As a song put it: "All the fitness He requires is to feel your need of Him."

Can I out-sin Christ's faithfulness? Can I exhaust it? "Even if we are faithless, He remains faithful. He cannot deny Himself" (2 Timothy 2:13). And through the prophet: "I am the Lord, I do not change. Therefore you are not consumed, O sons of Jacob" (Malachi 3:6). Christ's faithfulness is as infinite as He is.

Psalm 36:5: "Your mercy, O Lord, is in the heavens; Your faithfulness reaches to the clouds." Psalm 119:90: "Your faithfulness endures to all generations." And when He comes again, the whole world will proclaim Him as the one called "Faithful and True" (Revelation 19:11).

Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8). He was faithful yesterday. He is faithful today. He will be faithful forever.

## Our Response: Living Faithfully

What are the implications of Christ's faithfulness to our own faithfulness?

First, **gratitude** — and in gratitude, faithfulness as well. Jesus calls us to follow Him, and when He does, it is not some half-hearted effort He wants. "No one having put his hand to the plow and looking back is fit for the kingdom of God" (Luke 9:62). But if this sounds daunting, be encouraged: the very Spirit who anointed Jesus to accomplish His mission lives in you.

Faithfulness to God and faithfulness to others — because that is the point Paul makes in Galatians 5.

Are you committed to spending time with Him in worship, private and family devotions, and prayer? Are you willing to submit to God's direction for your life no matter how difficult? Faithfulness means sticking with the gospel even when the world wants you to abandon it.

Are you a faithful spouse? Husbands, are you loving your wives as Christ loves the church? Wives, are you submitting to your husbands as to Christ? Are you a faithful parent — available, raising your children in God's nurture and admonition even when it is hard? Are you a faithful friend — are your friends closer to Christ because they are your friends? Are you a faithful church member — faithful in attendance, in prayer for your brothers and sisters?

There is not a single aspect of our lives where we are exempt from faithfulness. Give yourself to Christ, and everything else will fall from there. This Christ-centeredness will create a character in you that is devoted to others, dependable, trustworthy, true, and faithful. You will be a person that others entrust the China of their lives to.

Let us pray. Father, we thank you that you are a faithful God, and that that faithfulness is demonstrated in Christ. We pray that we would live out that faithfulness in our own lives. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
